text: Identifies the father's conditional promise and the son's marriage within 18 months as offer and acceptance forming a unilateral contract.
Recognizes the issue whether the son's marriage constitutes bargained-for consideration or is merely a condition on a gratuitous promise.
Explains that no signed writing exists between father and son, making the oral promise unenforceable under the marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds.
Analyzes the suretyship Statute of Frauds provision and concludes it likely does not apply because the promise was made to the son (debtor), not the creditor.
Evaluates promissory estoppel as a possible but weak exception to the Statute of Frauds due to weak inducement and reliance by the son.
Notes that the son's prior intent to marry the politician's daughter weakens inducement and reliance for consideration and promissory estoppel.
States that the father's unilateral mistake about the availability of a tax deduction does not excuse performance or affect enforceability absent express condition.
Concludes that the father's oral promise is unenforceable due to the marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds writing requirement and absence of writing.
Avoids material doctrinal error by not conflating the one-year provision with the marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds requirement.
Includes counterarguments such as the son's prior intent negating inducement and the possibility of promissory estoppel.
The response identifies the controlling legal issue raised by the facts.
The response addresses the principal counterargument or defense when relevant.
The response addresses the element of Main purpose of promisor.
